request.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?

title: Come to Me (France Joli song)
Love Machine (The Miracles song)
Bardeux
Lean into It
Extra Texture (Read All About It)
Jeff Dawson
Right Back Where We Started From (album)
Manu Gavassi (album)
Attitudes (band)
Welcome Back (John Sebastian album)
sentences: "Come to Me" is a #1 disco hit from 1979 performed by France Joli, who had recorded it at the age of fifteen with producer, Tony Green, who composed the song and briefly sings on it.
The track also features the famed Philadelphia session vocalists, The Sweethearts of Sigma Sound.
The song was introduced on the album "France Joli", which was released in the US on 17 April 1979 on Prelude, and rose to #26.
On the Billboard Hot 100.
"Come to Me" received a major boost on 7 July when Joli performed it as a last minute replacement for Donna Summer at a concert held on Fire Island, whose estimated audience numbered 5000.
"Come to Me" began a three-week reign atop the Billboard Hot Dance Club Play on 22 September 1979.

"Come to Me" peaked at #15 on the Hot 100 and at #36 on the R&B chart, a decent showing for a new artist, but also a symptom of the increasing disenchantment of Top 40 radio stations with disco music.
"Love Machine" is a 1975 single recorded by Motown group The Miracles, taken from their album "City of Angels".
This song was a #1 Pop smash on the "Billboard Hot 100", and the biggest-selling hit single of The Miracles' career.
This single was one of two Billboard Hot 100 Top 20 hits recorded by The Miracles with Billy Griffin as lead vocalist; the other is 1973's "Do It Baby".
Griffin had replaced Miracles founder Smokey Robinson as lead singer in 1972.
The song features a growling vocal by Miracle Bobby Rogers, with group baritone Ronnie White repeating ""Yeah Baby"" throughout the song.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.
Lean into It is the second studio album by the American rock supergroup Mr. Big, released in 1991.

The band's breakthrough release, "Lean into It" peaked at number 15 on the "Billboard" 200 charts, while the single "To Be with You" became the band's first and only song to hit number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The follow-up single, "Just Take My Heart", was another Top 40 hit, peaking at number 16 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975.
It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple.
The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album.
The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the criticism generated by these projects.
Jeff Dawson is a Canadian record producer, song writer, engineer, and mixer.
Vancouver-based Jeff Dawson developed and produced the breakthrough self-titled second album for Daniel Powter.
After working on Daniel's songs and helping develop the project's sound over 3 years, Warner Bros. Chairman/CEO Tom Whalley took one listen and knew he had something extraordinary.
Daniel's single "Bad Day" was #1 for 5 weeks on Billboard Hot 100 of 2006 and has become an incredible world wide hit with almost 2.2 million downloads and nearly 3 million albums sold.
Dawson produced State of Shock's hit single "Money Honey" (certified platinum in Canada) cementing Top 10 status at multiple Canadian radio formats, including Rock, CHR, and Hot AC.

Along with becoming the #1 most played/requested song at Satellite radio selling 40,000 copies prior to its US release, surpassing platinum status for digital sales with over 51,000 sold, "Money Honey" also held down the #1 post on the Canadian all formats Radio Chart for two months, spending close to a year on the Canadian Billboard Hot 100 singles Chart.

Right Back Where We Started From is the debut album by British R&B and soul music singer Maxine Nightingale The title track was first released as a single in the autumn of 1975 and reached #8 in the UK .
In the US it reached #5 in Adult Contemporary, #9 in Dance Music/Club Play, #46 in R&B singles and #2 in the Billboard Hot 100.
It was the success of this single to which prompted her to return to London to complete a Right Back Where We Started From album.
The album was released in the US early 1976 to enthusiastic reaction, reaching #65 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Another single from the album, “Gotta Be the One”, charted at #53 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Manu Gavassi is the debut album of Brazilian singer-songwriter Manu Gavassi.
The album contains thirteen tracks, the sound is defined with strong influence on teen pop and pop rock.
The first single from the album was "Garoto Errado".
The song was released in June 11, 2011 and peaked at number 22 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The second single from the album was "Planos Impossíveis" and was released in January 25, 2011.
The song received positive reviews from critics and peaked at number 26 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The album was produced by Rick Bonadio and released through Universal Music Brazil's subsidiary, Midas Music, on October 12, 2010.
Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner.
Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".
One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976.
James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and that version reached #61 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1978.
Welcome Back is an album by American singer/songwriter John Sebastian, released in 1976 (see 1976 in music).
It reached number 79 on The Billboard 200 chart.
Two singles were released from the album with the title song "Welcome Back" reaching number one on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and Adult Contemporary charts.
It also reached number 93 on the Country Singles chart.
The second single, "Hideaway" peaked at number 95 on the Billboard Hot 100.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
hop1_docs: Klique | Klique was an American R&B trio, consisting of Howard Huntsberry, Isaac Suthers and his sister, Deborah Suthers.  They released four albums, starting with "It's Winning Time" in 1981, concluding with "Love Cycles" in 1985.  Their only song to make the US "Billboard" Hot 100 chart was a cover version of Jackie Wilson's 1960 hit, "Stop Doggin' Me Around," which reached #50 on the Hot 100 and #2 on the US "Billboard" R&B chart in 1983.  In total, they had nine songs on "Billboard"' s R&B chart.

Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song) | "Break It to Me Gently" is a song written by Carole Bayer Sager and Marvin Hamlisch which was an R&B hit for Aretha Franklin in 1977.  Released from the "Sweet Passion" album, it reached Number 1 on the Hot Soul Singles chart in June 1977.  The song's success was only faintly reflected on the "Billboard" Hot 100 with a Number 85 peak, before dropping out of the Hot 100 after two weeks.  "Break It to Me Gently" would be Franklin's final Atlantic single to appear on the Hot 100 - from which she would be absent until 1980.

Jibbs | Jovan Campbell (born November 13, 1990), better known by his stage name Jibbs, is an American rapper.  He had one top ten single on the "Billboard" Hot 100, which was his debut single "Chain Hang Low".  Jibbs' only other song that charted on the "Billboard" Hot 100 is "King Kong" (featuring Chamillionaire).  Jibbs is currently working on his next studio album, "Ten Years Later", to be released in 2017.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Tyga discography | The discography of Tyga, an American rapper, consists of four studio albums, two compilation albums, fourteen mixtapes, eight singles (including four as a featured artist) and forty-eight music videos.  In 2008, Tyga released his first studio album, "No Introduction", on the record label Decaydance Records.  The album featured the single "Coconut Juice", which features singer Travie McCoy; the song peaked at number 94 on the US "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming Tyga's first song to appear on the chart.  In 2010, Tyga and American singer Chris Brown released the collaborative mixtape "Fan of a Fan", which included the single "Deuces": the song peaked at number 14 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and became Tyga's first song to chart on the US Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart, peaking at number thirty.  Tyga also appeared on the song "Loyalty", a single by fellow rapper Birdman, and collaborated with rapper Lil Wayne on the non-album single "I'm on It", both of which failed to chart on the "Billboard" Hot 100.

Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

Lil Wayne singles discography | American rapper Lil Wayne has released 200 singles – including 44 as a lead artist – and twelve promotional singles. Lil Wayne attained his first singles chart entry in 1999 as a featured artist on Hot Boys member Juvenile's single "Back That Azz Up", which peaked at number 19 on the United States "Billboard" Hot 100 and became a top ten hit on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs and Hot Rap Songs charts.  Wayne later released his debut solo studio album "Tha Block Is Hot" in November 1999.  Its title track and lead single, which features B.G. and Juvenile, reached number 65 on the "Billboard" Hot 100. " Lights Out" followed in December 2000 and produced the singles "Get Off the Corner", "Everything" and "Shine". " Way of Life", the lead single from Wayne's third studio album "500 Degreez", peaked at number 71 on the Hot 100 and became a top 20 hit on the Hot Rap Songs chart.  In 2004, Wayne was featured on the single "Soldier" by American girl group Destiny's Child, which became his first top ten hit on the Hot 100 and enjoyed commercial success internationally.
hop1_titles: Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song)
Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Peter Frampton — [4] — 'gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! "'
- "Show Me the Way" — [4] — 'reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100'
- 'originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton"' — [4]
- Rod Stewart — [6] — 'for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".'
- "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" — [6] — 'The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.'
- 'spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977,' — [6]

Ambiguities:
- Which artist/album is meant by "album which reflects on his depressive state" — no passage mentions that phrase.
- Multiple 1976 Hot 100 candidates in the passages: Peter Frampton (passage [4]) vs Rod Stewart (passage [6]).
- Frampton album ambiguity: passage [4] mentions both the 1975 studio album "Frampton" and the 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! " (both referenced for "Show Me the Way").

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing identification of the album (title) and artist described as "reflects on his depressive state".
- Missing confirmation that that specific album (title/artist) had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Frampton Comes Alive!" (album) — to confirm album details and charting songs.
- Page: "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)" — to confirm chart dates and album attribution.
- Page: "A Night on the Town (Rod Stewart album)" — to confirm album context and singles.
- Page: "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" (Rod Stewart song) — to confirm charting in 1976.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: Peter Frampton album Rod Stewart Billboard Hot 100 1976
hop2_docs: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Forever Young (Rod Stewart song) | "Forever Young" is the second single released by Rod Stewart from his "Out of Order" album in 1988.  The song was a Top 20 hit on the "Billboard" Hot 100, peaking at #12.

Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

The Motown Song | "The Motown Song" is a song performed by Rod Stewart and featuring The Temptations.  The song is from Stewart's 1991 album "Vagabond Heart".  The song peaked at #10 on the Billboard Hot 100 chart on September 21, 1991.  It also peaked at #3 on the Billboard Adult Contemporary chart.  It was written by Larry John McNally and was originally recorded by McNally for the soundtrack to the film "Quicksilver" in 1986.

I'm in You (song) | "I'm in You" is the hit title song released by Peter Frampton as a single from his album "I'm in You", released in 1977.  It rose to No. 2 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 and No. 1 on the "Cash Box" Top 100 and in Canada, becoming his most successful single.  The song was kept from reaching the top spot on "Billboard" by Andy Gibb's "I Just Want to Be Your Everything".  "I'm in You" is ranked as the 42nd biggest hit of 1977.

Some Guys Have All the Luck | "Some Guys Have All the Luck" is a song written by Jeff Fortgang, which has been a Top 40 hit on the "Billboard" Hot 100 twice, first by The Persuaders in 1973 reaching #39, then by Rod Stewart in 1984, whose version hit #10.  Stewart's version incorporated a vocal refrain from "Ain't Got No Home" by Clarence "Frogman" Henry.  The Shakers recorded it for their debut album "Yankee Reggae" (Elektra, 1976) and released the song as promo-only 45 rpm single.

Rod Stewart | Sir Roderick David "Rod" Stewart, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (born 10 January 1945) is a British rock singer and songwriter.  Born and raised in London, he is of Scottish and English ancestry.  Stewart is one of the best-selling music artists of all time, having sold over 100 million records worldwide.  He has had six consecutive number one albums in the UK and his tally of 62 UK hit singles includes 31 that reached the top ten, six of which gained the #1 position.  Stewart has had 16 top ten singles in the US, with four reaching #1 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  He was knighted in the 2016 Birthday Honours for services to music and charity.
